_note: The world bible. Every script is generated against this file and validated against it before TTS. Nothing enters canon without passing pipeline/canon.py validation. Dwij owns every taste decision here — the pipeline may PROPOSE additions (canon_additions in a script), it may never commit them unattended.

planet.name: Kelvara
planet.name_status: PROVISIONAL — Dwij vetoes or confirms before episode 1 publishes
planet.name_rule: The planet's name NEVER appears in a video title (config.format.planet_name_in_title=false). Titles sell the premise; the episode builds the IP. The name may be spoken inside the episode.
planet.designation: the fourth world of a quiet star
planet.truth_label: Original speculative documentary. The place is fictional; the principles are real.

physics._note: These constants are HARD. Every mechanism in every episode must be consistent with them. If a script needs a constant that isn't here, it must be added here first.

star.consequence: Daylight is warm, low-angle and dim by Earth standards — roughly late-afternoon light at noon. This is WHY the palette is amber and WHY vision is an expensive sense here (see biology.sensory_bias).

physics.consequence_gravity: Structures span further before they buckle; dust settles more slowly and stays airborne longer; humans and native fauna are taller and thinner-boned than Earth equivalents.
physics.consequence_day: Long, brutal afternoons. Thermal management — not cold — is the planet's defining engineering problem. This is the spine of the whole channel.

atmosphere.composition: N2 74%, O2 19%, Ar 5%, CO2 0.3%, trace SO2 near volcanic provinces
atmosphere.consequence: Breathable without assistance at low altitude. Thin enough that convective cooling is WEAK — radiators underperform Earth intuition by roughly a fifth. Every settlement's heat budget is tighter than it looks.

physics.water: Liquid, abundant in one hemisphere, scarce in the other. The scarcity line is the planet's central political fact.
physics.magnetosphere: Weak. Surface radiation is survivable but not ignorable; long-lived settlements go underground or under water — which is why almost every settlement in this atlas is buried, sunken or shaded.

atlas._note: The map grows with the library. Every episode opens with a locator on this atlas — that continuity IS the returning-viewer glue. Regions are revealed, never retconned.

regions.name: The Cinder Shelf
regions.description: A basalt plateau under a permanently amber sky. Daytime surface temperatures climb through the long afternoon and do not fall until the 15-hour night. Dust here is fine, volcanic, and biologically active.
regions.engineering_fact: Nothing survives on the surface. Everything that lives here lives underneath it.

regions.name: The Slow Sea
regions.description: A warm, shallow, biologically dense sea on the water hemisphere. Its tides are not driven by Vell alone — something in the water anticipates them.
regions.engineering_fact: The sea is the planet's heat sink, its larder, and — as later episodes establish — arguably one organism.
regions.reserved_for: The ecology episode 'This Ocean Is One Animal' — unlocks only AFTER six stress-test episodes (config.format.ecology_episode_unlocks_after).

atlas.unrevealed_regions_hint: Polar, equatorial dead zone, sky-plankton layer, the flooded shield — none of these may be referenced on screen until an episode reveals them.

settlements.name: Tehlmark Deep
settlements.premise: A city cut vertically into basalt to escape a surface that cooks. Eleven levels, the deepest 340 metres down.

systems.cooling: PRIMARY SYSTEM — a closed groundwater loop pulls waste heat from every level and dumps it into an aquifer 500 m below the lowest gallery.
systems.power: Geothermal, tapped from the same basalt that traps the heat. Elegant, and a trap: the power source and the heat problem are the same rock.
systems.air: Passive stack ventilation driven by the day/night temperature swing at the surface shafts.
systems.water: The cooling aquifer is ALSO the drinking supply, filtered. This single shared dependency is the hidden fault.
systems.food: Fungal galleries on level 9, fed on surface dust organics.

settlements.hidden_dependency: Cooling, drinking water and power all terminate in one aquifer. Nobody drew that on the same diagram until it was too late.
settlements.verdict_reason: A dust-borne organism (ORG-01) colonised the heat-exchanger surfaces, halved their conductivity over 60 days, and turned the city's own geothermal power into an oven it could not switch off.
settlements.status: abandoned — sealed, not destroyed. Available for a future return episode.

organisms.name: cinder film
organisms.biology: A colonial chemolithotroph — a mineral-eating biofilm, not a plant or an animal. It metabolises iron and sulphur compounds in volcanic dust and it thrives in exactly the temperature band that heat exchangers operate in (45-70 C).
organisms.real_world_basis: Earth analogue: Acidithiobacillus / Sulfolobus-type extremophile biofilms, and the industrial biofouling of heat exchangers, which is a real and expensive engineering problem on Earth.
organisms.visual_signature: A matte, rust-black crust with a faint iridescent bloom where it is actively growing. It is never rendered as a monster and never given a face.
organisms._why: Deliberate. Colonial/amorphous organisms carry ZERO cross-episode anatomy-continuity debt — the single hardest technical risk in an AI-rendered persistent world. Early episodes bias toward organisms with no fixed body plan.

recurring_species_budget._note: Continuity risk control. Recurring species are expensive to keep visually consistent, so they are rationed.

verdict_record._note: The accumulating history of the world — the spine that makes the library compound. Also feeds the outcome-ratio tracker so the channel doesn't become collapse-monotony.

naming_rules.settlements: Hard consonants + a geographic noun: Tehlmark Deep, Corran Shaft, Ossul Reach. Never whimsical, never fantasy-elvish.
naming_rules.organisms: Working-class descriptive names, the way field biologists actually name things: cinder film, glass grazer, the tide. Latin binomials never appear — this is a field report, not a taxonomy paper.
naming_rules.regions: Plain English with one unsettling adjective: The Cinder Shelf, The Slow Sea.
forbidden: names that sound like a fantasy novel

hard_rules: The place is fictional; the principles are real. Every mechanism must have a real-world analogue that a curious viewer can go and verify.
No real people, no real places, no real events, no religion, no politics.
Every episode is openly labelled speculative fiction in the first 10 seconds and in the description.
Nothing contradicts this file. If a script needs to bend canon, the script loses.
Viewer canon = votes on creator-designed options ONLY. Any submitted name or design requires explicit written permission and a licence before it enters this file.
The planet's name never appears in a video title.
